1:
"Finally, my brethren, rejoice in the Lord. To write the same things to you, to me indeed is not grievous, but for you it is safe." The witnesses note the call to rejoice in the Lord and the safety of repeated warning. On "finally, my brethren, rejoice in the Lord," the grammatical voices note the keynote. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, turning ("finally") toward the close, sounds the keynote of the epistle: "rejoice in the Lord" — joy not in circumstances but in the Lord himself, the abiding source of gladness.

On "rejoice in the Lord," the eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the ground: the believer's joy is grounded in Christ, independent of outward things; even in prison Paul bids them rejoice, for the Lord is the unfailing fountain of joy.

On "to write the same things... for you it is safe," the witnesses note the repetition. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's readiness to repeat his warnings: to "write the same things" (perhaps the warnings against the Judaizers, or the call to joy) is "not grievous (not irksome)" to him, "but for you it is safe (a safeguard)" — repetition guards them from error. The verse displays the call to rejoice in the Lord and the safety of repeated warning.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul sounds the keynote "rejoice in the Lord," joy grounded in the Lord himself not circumstances, the abiding source of gladness (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom); and his readiness to "write the same things," not grievous to him but "safe" for them, repetition guarding against error (Barnes, Calvin). The verse displays joy and safe repetition. The verse marks the keynote: rejoice in the Lord. The verse displays the ground: the Lord himself. The verse displays the repetition: the same things. The verse displays the safety: a safeguard. The call displays joy in the Lord and the safety of repeated warning. The verse displays the rejoicing: in the Lord. The verse displays the writing: repeated. The verse displays the safety: for them. The call to rejoice in the Lord and the safety of repeated warning displays the keynote of the epistle and Paul's pastoral care: he bids the Philippians find their joy in the Lord himself, and counts it no burden but their safeguard to repeat his needful warnings. The verse displays the joy of the Lord, sharpened by a warning against false teachers (v.2).
2:
"Beware of dogs, beware of evil workers, beware of the concision." The brief verse notes the threefold warning against the Judaizing teachers. The grammatical voices note the sharp triple charge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's vehement, threefold "beware" against the false teachers: "beware of dogs" — the Judaizers, called "dogs" (the term the Jews used for Gentiles, now turned on them as impudent, unclean, snapping at the truth).

On "evil workers," the witnesses note the second. Barnes and JFB note "evil workers" — those who, though they labour, work evil; their activity in spreading legalism harms rather than builds the church.

On "the concision," the witnesses note the pun. Barnes and Wesley note the cutting wordplay: "the concision (katatome — a mere cutting, mutilation)" — Paul refuses the Judaizers the honourable name "circumcision (peritome)," calling their rite a mere fleshly mutilation, now that the true circumcision is of the heart. The verse displays the threefold warning against the Judaizing teachers.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's vehement threefold "beware" — "dogs" (the Judaizers, the term for Gentiles turned on them as unclean and snapping), "evil workers" (those whose labour works evil), and "the concision" (katatome, a mere mutilation, denying them the name "circumcision," Barnes, Robertson, JFB, Wesley). The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the first: dogs. The verse displays the second: evil workers. The verse displays the third: the concision. The verse displays the pun: cutting, not circumcision. The warning displays Paul's sharp charge against the legalists. The verse displays the dogs: the Judaizers. The verse displays the workers: evil. The verse displays the concision: mutilation. The threefold warning against the Judaizing teachers displays Paul's vehement guarding of the gospel: with biting force he brands the legalists as dogs, evil workers, and the mere "concision" — a fleshly mutilation, not the true circumcision — that the Philippians may beware of them. The verse displays the warning against the false, against which Paul sets the true circumcision (v.3). The vehemence of the threefold warning is not the language of personal spite but of pastoral alarm, for these false teachers struck at the very root of the gospel by binding the conscience again to the rite of the flesh; and so the apostle, who elsewhere could speak of his countrymen with tears, here turns their own proudest title against them, refusing to dignify a mere cutting of the body with the honourable name of the covenant sign, now that its true meaning has passed to the circumcision of the heart.
3:
"For we are the circumcision, which worship God in the spirit, and rejoice in Christ Jesus, and have no confidence in the flesh." The witnesses note that believers are the true circumcision, worshipping in the Spirit. On "we are the circumcision," the grammatical voices note the claim. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's striking assertion: "we (believers in Christ) are the circumcision" — the true circumcision is no longer the fleshly rite but the spiritual reality, the people of God marked inwardly by the Spirit (Romans 2:29).

On "which worship God in the spirit," the witnesses note the first mark. Barnes and Calvin note the first mark of the true circumcision: they "worship God in the spirit (by the Spirit, spiritually)" — true worship is inward and spiritual, not the outward ritual the Judaizers trusted.

On "rejoice in Christ Jesus, and have no confidence in the flesh," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the other two marks: they "rejoice (glory, boast) in Christ Jesus" (not in the law or themselves) and "have no confidence in the flesh" (in fleshly privileges, rites, or works) — the threefold mark of the spiritual people. The verse displays believers as the true circumcision.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "we (believers) are the circumcision," the true circumcision now the spiritual reality, marked inwardly by the Spirit (Romans 2:29, Barnes, Robertson); the threefold mark — "worship God in the spirit" (spiritual worship, not ritual), "rejoice (glory) in Christ Jesus" (not in self or law), and "have no confidence in the flesh" (no trust in fleshly privileges or works, Barnes, Calvin, Wesley). The verse displays the true circumcision. The verse marks the claim: we are the circumcision. The verse displays the first mark: worship in the spirit. The verse displays the second: rejoice in Christ. The verse displays the third: no confidence in the flesh. The claim displays believers as the spiritual people of God. The verse displays the circumcision: spiritual. The verse displays the worship: in the Spirit. The verse displays the boast: in Christ. That believers are the true circumcision, worshipping in the Spirit, displays the reality the Judaizers missed: the people of God are marked not by the fleshly rite but by spiritual worship, glorying in Christ Jesus, and placing no confidence in the flesh — the true circumcision of the heart. The verse displays the spiritual people, over against Paul's own fleshly credentials (vv.4-6).
4:
"Though I might also have confidence in the flesh. If any other man thinketh that he hath whereof he might trust in the flesh, I more:" The brief verse notes Paul's claim that he had more ground than any for confidence in the flesh. The grammatical voices note the concession. Barnes and Robertson note Paul anticipates an objection: he renounces confidence in the flesh not because he had none, for "I might also have confidence in the flesh" — he possessed every fleshly advantage the Judaizers boasted.

On "if any other man thinketh... I more," the witnesses note the challenge. Barnes and Henry note Paul's claim: "if any other man thinketh that he hath whereof he might trust in the flesh, I more" — whatever ground for fleshly confidence anyone could claim, Paul had more; no one could outdo him in Jewish privilege.

The Reformed Calvin notes the force: Paul's renunciation of fleshly confidence is the more striking because he had the most to renounce; he speaks not as one who had nothing, but as one who gave up everything. The verse displays Paul's claim that he had more ground than any for confidence in the flesh.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul anticipates the objection — he renounces fleshly confidence not for lack of it, for "I might also have confidence in the flesh," possessing every advantage the Judaizers boasted (Barnes, Robertson); "if any man thinketh... I more," whatever ground anyone could claim, Paul had more (Barnes, Henry); his renunciation the more striking for having the most to renounce (Calvin). The verse displays his fleshly credentials. The verse marks the concession: I might have confidence. The verse displays the challenge: I more. The verse displays the advantage: every privilege. The verse displays the force: most to renounce. The claim displays Paul outdoing all in fleshly grounds. The verse displays the confidence: possible. The verse displays the boast: surpassing. The verse displays the renunciation: the more striking. Paul's claim that he had more ground than any for confidence in the flesh displays the weight of his renunciation: he gave up fleshly confidence not because he lacked it, but because he possessed it beyond all the Judaizers — whatever they could boast, he could boast more. The verse displays the apostle's surpassing credentials, detailed in his sevenfold pedigree (vv.5-6).
5:
"Circumcised the eighth day, of the stock of Israel, of the tribe of Benjamin, an Hebrew of the Hebrews; as touching the law, a Pharisee;" The brief verse notes Paul's pedigree of Jewish privilege. The grammatical voices note the catalogue of inherited advantages. Barnes and Robertson note Paul lists his fleshly credentials: "circumcised the eighth day" (according to the law, not a proselyte circumcised later), "of the stock (race) of Israel" (a true-born Israelite), "of the tribe of Benjamin" (an honoured tribe), "an Hebrew of the Hebrews" (a pure-blooded Hebrew, of Hebrew parentage and speech).

On "as touching the law, a Pharisee," the witnesses note his attained standing. Barnes and Calvin note his personal attainment: "as touching the law, a Pharisee" — he belonged to the strictest sect, the most rigorous observers of the law; no higher legal standing existed among the Jews.

The grammatical-historical note the completeness: Paul's credentials covered both birth and choice — every advantage of Jewish descent and of religious zeal. The verse displays Paul's pedigree of Jewish privilege.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul lists his credentials — "circumcised the eighth day" (by the law, not a proselyte), "of the stock of Israel" (true-born), "of the tribe of Benjamin" (honoured tribe), "an Hebrew of the Hebrews" (pure-blooded, Hebrew parentage and speech, Barnes, Robertson); "as touching the law, a Pharisee," of the strictest sect, the highest legal standing (Barnes, Calvin). The verse displays his pedigree. The verse marks the birth: circumcised, Israel, Benjamin. The verse displays the descent: Hebrew of Hebrews. The verse displays the attainment: a Pharisee. The verse displays the completeness: birth and choice. The pedigree displays Paul's every Jewish advantage. The verse displays the circumcision: by the law. The verse displays the tribe: Benjamin. The verse displays the sect: Pharisee. Paul's pedigree of Jewish privilege displays the surpassing ground he had for fleshly confidence: circumcised by the law, a true-born Israelite of Benjamin, a pure Hebrew, and by choice a Pharisee of the strictest sect — every advantage of birth and zeal. The verse displays the credentials of descent and sect, completed by his zeal and blamelessness (v.6).
6:
"Concerning zeal, persecuting the church; touching the righteousness which is in the law, blameless." The brief verse notes Paul's zeal and his legal blamelessness. The grammatical voices note the zeal. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's further credential: "concerning zeal, persecuting the church" — his religious zeal was so intense that it drove him to persecute the church; by the standard of zealous Judaism, his persecution was a mark of devotion.

On "touching the righteousness which is in the law, blameless," the witnesses note the legal standing. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's claim: "touching the righteousness which is in the law (the outward, external righteousness of legal observance), blameless" — measured by the law's external requirements, no one could fault him; he kept its rules irreproachably.

The Reformed Calvin notes the irony: by every human and legal measure Paul was a model — yet all this he counted loss; the very righteousness men prized he renounced for Christ. The verse displays Paul's zeal and his legal blamelessness.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "concerning zeal, persecuting the church," his intense religious zeal driving him to persecute, by Judaism's standard a mark of devotion (Barnes, Robertson); "touching the righteousness which is in the law (external legal observance), blameless," irreproachable by the law's outward requirements (Barnes, Calvin); yet all this counted loss, the very righteousness men prized renounced for Christ (Calvin). The verse displays his zeal and blamelessness. The verse marks the zeal: persecuting the church. The verse displays the righteousness: of the law. The verse displays the standing: blameless. The verse displays the irony: all counted loss. The credentials display Paul's zeal and legal perfection. The verse displays the zeal: intense. The verse displays the law: kept. The verse displays the blamelessness: outward. Paul's zeal and his legal blamelessness display the summit of his fleshly credentials: his zeal was so fierce it persecuted the church, and by the law's external righteousness he was blameless — the very pinnacle of Jewish attainment, which he would yet count as loss. The verse displays the height of his fleshly righteousness, all reckoned loss for Christ (v.7).
7:
"But what things were gain to me, those I counted loss for Christ." The witnesses note Paul counting his former gains as loss for Christ. On "what things were gain to me," the grammatical voices note the reckoning. Barnes and Robertson note the great reversal: "what things were gain (advantages, things he had reckoned as profit — his pedigree, zeal, and legal righteousness) to me" — all that he once counted as spiritual capital.

On "those I counted loss for Christ," the witnesses note the surrender. Barnes and Calvin note the deliberate exchange: "those I counted (have reckoned, and still reckon) loss for Christ" — he now regards all his former gains not merely as worthless but as positive loss, hindrances to be cast away for the sake of Christ.

The eastern Chrysostom notes the radical change: what once seemed his greatest assets Paul now sees as liabilities, for they had stood between him and Christ; the gain became loss when set against the surpassing worth of Christ. The verse displays Paul counting his former gains as loss for Christ.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "what things were gain (advantages, reckoned as profit) to me" — all Paul once counted as spiritual capital (Barnes, Robertson); "those I counted (and still count) loss for Christ," now regarding them not merely worthless but positive loss, hindrances cast away for Christ (Barnes, Calvin); what seemed his greatest assets now seen as liabilities standing between him and Christ (Chrysostom). The verse displays gain counted loss. The verse marks the gains: pedigree and righteousness. The verse displays the reckoning: counted loss. The verse displays the cause: for Christ. The verse displays the change: assets to liabilities. The reckoning displays Paul exchanging all gain for Christ. The verse displays the gain: former. The verse displays the loss: now. The verse displays the Christ: the cause. Paul counting his former gains as loss for Christ displays the great reversal of his conversion: all that he once reckoned as profit — his Jewish privilege, zeal, and legal righteousness — he now counts as loss, casting it away for the surpassing worth of Christ. The verse displays the renouncing of all for Christ, intensified to counting all things dung (v.8).
8:
"Yea doubtless, and I count all things but loss for the excellency of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord: for whom I have suffered the loss of all things, and do count them but dung, that I may win Christ," The witnesses note Paul counting all things but dung that he may win Christ. On "I count all things but loss for the excellency of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord," the grammatical voices note the surpassing worth. Barnes and Robertson note Paul broadens the reckoning: not only his Jewish gains but "all things" he counts loss "for the excellency (the surpassing worth, the supreme value) of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord" — the knowing of Christ outweighs everything.

On "I have suffered the loss of all things, and do count them but dung," the witnesses note the cost and the contempt. Barnes and Calvin note that Paul has actually "suffered the loss of all things" (forfeited all his former advantages) and counts them "but dung (skybala — refuse, filth, what is thrown to dogs)" — not merely worthless but loathsome, to be cast away with disgust.

On "that I may win Christ," the Reformed Calvin notes the aim: the whole purpose is "that I may win (gain, acquire) Christ" — to possess Christ himself is the prize for which all else is gladly lost. The verse displays Paul counting all things but dung that he may win Christ.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul counts "all things" loss "for the excellency (surpassing worth) of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord," the knowing of Christ outweighing all (Barnes, Robertson); he has "suffered the loss of all things" and counts them "but dung (refuse, filth)," loathsome to be cast away (Barnes, Calvin); the aim "that I may win (gain) Christ," to possess Christ the prize (Calvin). The verse displays all counted dung. The verse marks the worth: the excellency of knowing Christ. The verse displays the loss: of all things. The verse displays the contempt: but dung. The verse displays the aim: win Christ. The reckoning displays all cast away to gain Christ. The verse displays the knowledge: surpassing. The verse displays the things: dung. The verse displays the Christ: won. Paul counting all things but dung that he may win Christ displays the supreme value he places on Christ: for the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus his Lord, he counts all things — and has actually forfeited all — as mere refuse, gladly cast away that he may gain Christ himself. The verse displays the supreme worth of Christ, sought as the true righteousness (v.9).
9:
"And be found in him, not having mine own righteousness, which is of the law, but that which is through the faith of Christ, the righteousness which is of God by faith:" The witnesses note the righteousness of God by faith, not one's own of the law. On "be found in him," the grammatical voices note the union. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's deepest aim: "be found in him (in Christ, united to him, clothed in him)" — to be discovered, at the judgment and now, as one who is in Christ, not standing on his own merits.

On "not having mine own righteousness, which is of the law, but... the righteousness which is of God by faith," the witnesses note the great contrast. Barnes, Calvin, and the Reformed note the heart of justification: Paul renounces "mine own righteousness, which is of the law (the righteousness of works, of legal observance, that one earns)" and embraces instead "that which is through the faith of Christ, the righteousness which is of God by faith" — a righteousness not produced by self but given by God, received by faith in Christ. The Reformed Calvin presses that this righteousness is imputed — Christ's righteousness reckoned to the believer, not his own; the believer is justified by a righteousness wholly outside himself, freely accounted to him.

The witnesses note the exclusion: no part of justifying righteousness is the believer's own; all is of God, through faith. The verse displays the righteousness of God by faith.

The witnesses agree the great contrast: Paul renounces "mine own righteousness, which is of the law" (the righteousness of works he earns) and embraces "the righteousness which is of God by faith" (through the faith of Christ) — a righteousness not produced by self but given by God and received by faith, that he may "be found in Christ," clothed in him, not on his own merits (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). Calvin presses that this righteousness is imputed — Christ's own righteousness reckoned to the believer, wholly outside himself, freely accounted to him; the foundation of the doctrine of justification by faith alone. The verse displays the righteousness of God by faith. The verse marks the union: found in him. The verse displays the renounced: own righteousness of the law. The verse displays the embraced: God's righteousness by faith. The verse displays the doctrine: imputation. The righteousness displays justification by a righteousness not our own. The verse displays the law-righteousness: rejected. The verse displays the faith-righteousness: received. The verse displays the gift: of God. The righteousness of God by faith, not one's own of the law, displays the heart of the gospel: Paul renounces all righteousness of his own works and is found in Christ, clothed in the righteousness that comes from God and is received by faith — Christ's righteousness imputed to the believer, freely accounted, not earned. The verse displays justification by faith, the ground of Paul's whole hope, that he may know Christ (v.10).
10:
"That I may know him, and the power of his resurrection, and the fellowship of his sufferings, being made conformable unto his death;" The witnesses note Paul's desire to know Christ, his resurrection power, and the fellowship of his sufferings. On "that I may know him, and the power of his resurrection," the grammatical voices note the experiential knowledge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's aim in being found in Christ: "that I may know him (experientially, intimately) and the power of his resurrection" — to know not facts about Christ but Christ himself, and the living power that raised him, working in the believer for new life.

On "the fellowship of his sufferings," the witnesses note the participation. Barnes and Calvin note the further desire: "the fellowship (koinonia — sharing, communion) of his sufferings" — to share in Christ's sufferings, to suffer with and for him, entering into communion with him through affliction.

On "being made conformable unto his death," the Reformed Calvin notes the conformity: "being made conformable (conformed, shaped) unto his death" — dying to sin and self as Christ died, and willing even to share the manner of his death; the believer is shaped into Christ's likeness through suffering and dying. The verse displays Paul's desire to know Christ and the fellowship of his sufferings.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's aim "that I may know him (intimately) and the power of his resurrection," knowing Christ himself and the living power that raised him, working for new life (Barnes, Robertson); "the fellowship (communion) of his sufferings," sharing in Christ's sufferings, suffering with and for him (Barnes, Calvin); "being made conformable unto his death," dying to sin and self as Christ died, shaped into his likeness (Calvin). The verse displays knowing Christ. The verse marks the knowledge: know him. The verse displays the power: of his resurrection. The verse displays the fellowship: of his sufferings. The verse displays the conformity: to his death. The desire displays Paul sharing Christ's power and sufferings. The verse displays the knowing: experiential. The verse displays the resurrection: its power. The verse displays the sufferings: shared. Paul's desire to know Christ, his resurrection power, and the fellowship of his sufferings displays the goal of being found in him: to know Christ himself experientially, to feel the power of his resurrection, and to share in his sufferings — being conformed even to his death, shaped into his likeness. The verse displays the believer's union with Christ in death and resurrection, reaching toward the final resurrection (v.11).
11:
"If by any means I might attain unto the resurrection of the dead." The brief verse notes Paul's aspiration to attain the resurrection of the dead. The grammatical voices note the goal. Barnes and Robertson note that the goal of knowing Christ and sharing his death is "if by any means I might attain unto the resurrection of the dead" — the blessed resurrection of the just (exanastasis — the out-resurrection, the rising of believers to glory).

On "if by any means," the witnesses note the expression. Barnes and Wesley note that "if by any means" expresses not doubt of the fact (the resurrection is sure) but humility and earnest striving — the intensity of his pursuit and the sense of the goal's greatness, not uncertainty of attaining it.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the manner: the conditional form implies no uncertainty of the outcome but the difficulty and earnestness of the attainment; Paul presses on with humble diligence toward the glorious resurrection. The verse displays Paul's aspiration to attain the resurrection of the dead.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the goal "if by any means I might attain unto the resurrection of the dead," the blessed resurrection of the just to glory (Barnes, Robertson); "if by any means" expressing not doubt of the fact but humility and earnest striving, the intensity of his pursuit (Barnes, Wesley); the conditional form implying difficulty and earnestness, not uncertainty (Wesley). The verse displays the aspiration. The verse marks the goal: the resurrection of the dead. The verse displays the manner: if by any means. The verse displays the humility: earnest striving. The verse displays the certainty: not doubt but diligence. The aspiration displays Paul pressing toward the glorious resurrection. The verse displays the resurrection: of the just. The verse displays the striving: earnest. The verse displays the goal: glory. Paul's aspiration to attain the resurrection of the dead displays the crown of his pursuit: that he might, through knowing Christ and sharing his death, attain the blessed resurrection of the just to glory — the "if by any means" marking not doubt but humble, earnest striving. The verse displays the goal of the resurrection, toward which Paul presses though not yet attaining (v.12).
12:
"Not as though I had already attained, either were already perfect: but I follow after, if that I may apprehend that for which also I am apprehended of Christ Jesus." The witnesses note Paul disclaiming attainment and pressing on — the crux of "perfect." On "not as though I had already attained, either were already perfect," interpreters divide over "perfect." The grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and the Reformed Calvin take it as Paul's denial that he has reached spiritual or moral perfection in this life — he has not yet been made complete, free from sin; Robertson notes Paul "pointedly denies" any absolute perfection attainable here. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke takes "perfect" (teteleiomai) in the racing/athletic sense — Paul has not yet completed his course or won the prize (by martyrdom, the consummation), and the words say nothing of indwelling sin; Wesley distinguishes the "perfect" (fit for the race, v.15) from the "perfected" (crowned, glorified), which Paul is not yet.

On "but I follow after... that I may apprehend," the witnesses note the pursuit. Barnes and Robertson note the racing image: Paul "follows after (pursues, presses on, keeps up the chase) that I may apprehend (lay hold of, seize)" the prize for which "I am apprehended (laid hold of, seized) of Christ Jesus" — at his conversion Christ grasped him with a view to this very prize.

The witnesses note the humility: Paul, the great apostle, claims no perfection but presses on toward the goal. The verse displays Paul disclaiming attainment and pressing on.

The witnesses agree the pursuit and humility: Paul disclaims having "already attained" the prize and presses on, pursuing it that he may lay hold of that for which Christ laid hold of him at his conversion (Barnes, Robertson). But "neither were already perfect" divides them: Barnes, Robertson, and Calvin take it as Paul's denial of attainable sinless/moral perfection in this life (Robertson: he "pointedly denies" absolute perfection here, knew nothing of sudden absolute perfection); Clarke takes "perfect" in the racing sense — not yet having completed his course or won the crown by martyrdom, saying nothing of indwelling sin; Wesley distinguishes "perfect" (fit for the race) from "perfected" (glorified), which Paul is not yet. The verse displays the press toward the goal. The verse marks the disclaimer: not already perfect. The verse displays the debate: moral perfection denied, or the racing sense. The verse displays the pursuit: I follow after. The verse displays the grasp: apprehended of Christ. The disclaimer displays Paul pressing on, claiming no perfection. The verse displays the attainment: not yet. The verse displays the chase: continued. The verse displays the Christ: who seized him. Paul disclaiming attainment and pressing on displays the humility of the great apostle and a perfection crux: he claims neither to have won the prize nor to be "already perfect" — read by most as the denial of attainable sinless perfection in this life, by Clarke as not yet completing the course, by Wesley as not yet glorified — but presses on to lay hold of that for which Christ laid hold of him. The verse displays the believer's onward striving, the one thing Paul does (vv.13-14).
13:
"Brethren, I count not myself to have apprehended: but this one thing I do, forgetting those things which are behind, and reaching forth unto those things which are before," The witnesses note Paul's single-minded forgetting the past and reaching forward. On "I count not myself to have apprehended," the grammatical voices note the renewed disclaimer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul repeats his humility: "I count not myself to have apprehended (to have laid hold of the prize, to have arrived)" — he does not reckon himself to have reached the goal.

On "this one thing I do," the witnesses note the concentration. Barnes and Chrysostom note Paul's singleness of aim: "this one thing I do" — his whole energy is focused on a single pursuit; the secret of his life is concentration on one object.

On "forgetting those things which are behind, and reaching forth unto those things which are before," the grammatical-historical note the racing image: like a runner, Paul is "forgetting (leaving behind, not dwelling on) those things which are behind (past attainments and failures alike)" and "reaching forth (stretching out, straining forward) unto those things which are before" — the body bent toward the goal, all attention on what lies ahead. The verse displays Paul's single-minded forgetting the past and reaching forward.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul repeats "I count not myself to have apprehended (the prize, the goal)," not reckoning himself arrived (Barnes, Robertson); "this one thing I do," his whole energy concentrated on a single pursuit (Barnes, Chrysostom); the racing image — "forgetting those things which are behind (past attainments and failures) and reaching forth (straining forward) unto those before," bent toward the goal (grammatical-historical). The verse displays forgetting and reaching. The verse marks the disclaimer: not apprehended. The verse displays the concentration: this one thing. The verse displays the forgetting: what is behind. The verse displays the reaching: what is before. The pursuit displays Paul straining single-mindedly toward the goal. The verse displays the past: forgotten. The verse displays the future: reached for. The verse displays the focus: one thing. Paul's single-minded forgetting the past and reaching forward displays the secret of his pursuit: not reckoning himself to have arrived, he concentrates all his energy on one thing — leaving behind both past attainments and failures, and straining forward, like a runner, toward what lies ahead. The verse displays the one-pointed striving, pressing toward the prize (v.14).
14:
"I press toward the mark for the prize of the high calling of God in Christ Jesus." The witnesses note Paul pressing toward the mark for the prize of the high calling. On "I press toward the mark," the grammatical voices note the effort. Barnes and Robertson note the racing image completed: "I press (run hard, pursue with all my might) toward the mark (the goal-post, the line that marks the finish)" — Paul drives toward the goal with concentrated, strenuous effort.

On "for the prize of the high calling," the witnesses note the reward. Barnes and Calvin note the object: "the prize (the victor's reward, the crown) of the high (or upward) calling of God" — the heavenly reward to which God calls believers, the crown of eternal life and glory at the goal.

On "of God in Christ Jesus," the grammatical-historical note the source: the high calling is "of God" (God calls) and "in Christ Jesus" (through and in Christ) — the calling and the prize alike come from God through Christ. The verse displays Paul pressing toward the mark for the prize.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the racing image completed — "I press (run hard) toward the mark (the goal-post)," driving toward the goal with strenuous effort (Barnes, Robertson); "for the prize (the victor's crown) of the high (upward) calling of God," the heavenly reward to which God calls, eternal glory (Barnes, Calvin); "of God in Christ Jesus," the calling and prize from God through Christ (grammatical-historical). The verse displays pressing toward the mark. The verse marks the effort: I press. The verse displays the goal: the mark. The verse displays the reward: the prize. The verse displays the calling: high, of God in Christ. The pursuit displays Paul straining toward the heavenly prize. The verse displays the pressing: hard. The verse displays the prize: the crown. The verse displays the calling: upward. Paul pressing toward the mark for the prize of the high calling displays the goal of all his striving: like a runner straining for the finish, he drives toward the goal to win the prize — the heavenly crown of God's upward calling in Christ Jesus, the reward of eternal glory. The verse displays the heavenly aim of the Christian race, the mind of the mature (v.15).
15:
"Let us therefore, as many as be perfect, be thus minded: and if in any thing ye be otherwise minded, God shall reveal even this unto you." The witnesses note the call for the mature to be thus minded — with the meaning of "perfect." On "as many as be perfect, be thus minded," interpreters note the sense of "perfect." The grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and Clarke take "perfect (teleioi)" here as the mature, the full-grown, the spiritually adult — not the absolute perfection denied in v.12, but relative perfection (Robertson: "relative perfection, not the absolute... so pointedly denied in verse 12"); Clarke: thoroughly instructed, adults in Christian knowledge. Ellicott adds a touch of irony — aimed at those who claim already to be perfect, bidding them prove it by a consciousness of their imperfection. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads "perfect" as those fit for the race, strong in faith.

On "be thus minded," the witnesses note the charge. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's exhortation: "be thus minded" — let the mature share this same mind, pressing on as Paul does, never content but always reaching forward.

On "if in any thing ye be otherwise minded, God shall reveal even this unto you," the Reformed Calvin notes the patience: where any sincerely differ or fall short in understanding, Paul does not condemn but trusts that "God shall reveal even this" — God will in time correct and teach them; truth is to be waited for with humility. The verse displays the call for the mature to be thus minded.

The witnesses agree the charge and the patience: let the "perfect" share Paul's mind of pressing on, and where any differ or fall short, God will in time reveal the truth to them (Barnes, Calvin, Robertson). And they agree "perfect" here means the mature, full-grown, spiritually adult — relative, not the absolute perfection denied in v.12 (Barnes, Robertson, Clarke); Ellicott adds a note of irony toward those who claim to be already perfect, who prove it best by feeling their imperfection; Wesley reads "perfect" as fit for the race, strong in faith. The verse displays the mind of the mature. The verse marks the call: as many as be perfect. The verse displays the meaning: mature, not absolute. The verse displays the charge: be thus minded. The verse displays the patience: God shall reveal. The call displays the mature pressing on, the differing taught by God. The verse displays the perfect: mature. The verse displays the mind: pressing on. The verse displays the revelation: of God. The call for the mature to be thus minded displays Paul's exhortation and gentleness: let the spiritually grown — "perfect" in the sense of mature, not the absolute perfection he disclaimed — share his mind of ever pressing on, and where any sincerely differ, God himself will in time reveal the truth to them. The verse displays the mind of the mature and patience with the differing, walking by the same rule (v.16).
16:
"Nevertheless, whereto we have already attained, let us walk by the same rule, let us mind the same thing." The brief verse notes the call to walk by the same rule already attained. The grammatical voices note the agreement. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's practical conclusion: "whereto we have already attained (so far as we have advanced, in the truth we share) let us walk by the same rule (kanon — the same standard, line, or measure)" — believers are to keep in step, ordering their lives by the truth they hold in common.

On "let us mind the same thing," the witnesses note the unity. Barnes and Calvin note the call to harmony: "let us mind (be intent on, set our minds on) the same thing" — united in aim and in the pursuit of the goal, not divided over the lesser matters on which they may yet differ.

The grammatical-historical note the balance: while waiting for fuller light on points of difference (v.15), believers are meanwhile to live united in what they already know and share. The verse displays the call to walk by the same rule already attained.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "whereto we have already attained (the truth we share) let us walk by the same rule (standard)," keeping in step, ordering life by common truth (Barnes, Robertson); "let us mind (set our minds on) the same thing," united in aim and pursuit, not divided over lesser matters (Barnes, Calvin); meanwhile living united in what they already know (grammatical-historical). The verse displays walking by the same rule. The verse marks the attainment: what we have reached. The verse displays the rule: the same standard. The verse displays the unity: mind the same thing. The verse displays the balance: united in the shared. The call displays believers keeping in step by common truth. The verse displays the rule: shared. The verse displays the walk: together. The verse displays the mind: united. The call to walk by the same rule already attained displays the practical unity Paul urges: so far as believers have advanced in the truth they hold in common, they are to keep in step and set their minds on the same thing — united in the shared while awaiting fuller light on points of difference. The verse displays the unity of the advancing, with a call to follow Paul's example (v.17).
17:
"Brethren, be followers together of me, and mark them which walk so as ye have us for an ensample." The brief verse notes the call to follow Paul's example and mark those who walk by it. The grammatical voices note the imitation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's bold but humble charge: "be followers together (joint-imitators) of me" — imitate me, as I imitate Christ (1 Corinthians 11:1); Paul offers his own life of pressing on as a pattern.

On "mark them which walk so as ye have us for an ensample," the witnesses note the wider pattern. Barnes and Henry note that they are also to "mark (observe, take note of, and follow) them which walk so" — those who live by the same example Paul set; the apostles and faithful believers are an "ensample (typos — pattern, model)" to be observed and copied.

The Reformed Calvin notes the warrant: Paul could set himself forward as a pattern because his life conformed to Christ; the call is not to vanity but to point them to a living model of the gospel. The verse displays the call to follow Paul's example.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's charge "be followers together (joint-imitators) of me," imitating him as he imitates Christ (1 Corinthians 11:1, Barnes, Robertson); also "mark (observe and follow) them which walk so," those living by the same example, the faithful an "ensample (pattern)" to copy (Barnes, Henry); Paul setting himself forward because his life conformed to Christ (Calvin). The verse displays following his example. The verse marks the imitation: followers of me. The verse displays the marking: those who walk so. The verse displays the pattern: an ensample. The verse displays the warrant: conformed to Christ. The call displays believers copying Paul and the faithful. The verse displays the following: of Paul. The verse displays the marking: of the faithful. The verse displays the example: to imitate. The call to follow Paul's example and mark those who walk by it displays the value of a living pattern: the Philippians are to imitate Paul, who imitates Christ, and to observe and follow all who walk by the same example — for a holy life is a model the gospel sets before the church. The verse displays the pattern of the faithful, against the enemies of the cross (vv.18-19).
18:
"For many walk, of whom I have told you often, and now tell you even weeping, that they are the enemies of the cross of Christ:" The brief verse notes Paul weeping over the enemies of the cross. The grammatical voices note the warning. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with the good example: "many walk (live, conduct themselves)" in a way Paul has "told you often, and now tell you even weeping" — there are many who live as "enemies of the cross of Christ."

On "even weeping," the witnesses note Paul's grief. Barnes and Chrysostom note the depth of his sorrow: Paul speaks "even weeping" — with tears; he does not denounce these enemies with cold severity but mourns over them, grieved at their ruin and their dishonour to Christ.

On "enemies of the cross of Christ," the grammatical-historical note the meaning: they are "enemies of the cross" — whether by their doctrine (rejecting the cross's offence, like the Judaizers) or, more likely here, by their life (sensual, earthly-minded, v.19), making the cross of no effect by their conduct. The verse displays Paul weeping over the enemies of the cross.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast with the good example — "many walk" in a way Paul has "told you often, and now tell you even weeping," living as "enemies of the cross of Christ" (Barnes, Robertson); his deep sorrow, speaking "even weeping," mourning over them rather than denouncing coldly (Barnes, Chrysostom); "enemies of the cross" by doctrine or, more likely, by their sensual, earthly life (grammatical-historical). The verse displays weeping over the enemies. The verse marks the many: walking ill. The verse displays the grief: even weeping. The verse displays the enemies: of the cross. The verse displays the cause: their life. The warning displays Paul mourning over the cross's enemies. The verse displays the walking: amiss. The verse displays the tears: of Paul. The verse displays the cross: opposed. Paul weeping over the enemies of the cross displays the tenderness behind his warning: he tells the Philippians, with tears, that many live as enemies of the cross of Christ — not denouncing them coldly but grieving over their ruin and the dishonour their conduct brings to the cross. The verse displays the apostle's grief, detailing the enemies' character (v.19).
19:
"Whose end is destruction, whose God is their belly, and whose glory is in their shame, who mind earthly things.)" The brief verse notes the destiny and character of the enemies of the cross. The grammatical voices note their end. Barnes and Robertson note the fourfold description of these enemies: "whose end is destruction (apoleia — ruin, perdition)" — their final destiny is everlasting ruin, the opposite of the salvation believers await.

On "whose God is their belly," the witnesses note their idolatry. Barnes and Calvin note that "their God is their belly" — they serve their appetites and sensual desires as their god; gluttony, indulgence, and self-gratification rule them in the place of God.

On "whose glory is in their shame, who mind earthly things," the grammatical-historical note the rest: they "glory (boast) in their shame" — taking pride in what should disgrace them; and "mind earthly things" — their whole thought and aim is set on the things of this world, not on heaven. The verse displays the destiny and character of the enemies of the cross.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fourfold description — "whose end is destruction (ruin, perdition)," their final destiny everlasting ruin (Barnes, Robertson); "whose God is their belly," serving their appetites as their god (Barnes, Calvin); "whose glory is in their shame," boasting in what should disgrace them; "who mind earthly things," set wholly on this world (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the enemies' character. The verse marks the end: destruction. The verse displays the god: their belly. The verse displays the glory: in their shame. The verse displays the mind: earthly things. The description displays the cross's enemies bound for ruin. The verse displays the destruction: their end. The verse displays the belly: their god. The verse displays the earthly: their mind. The destiny and character of the enemies of the cross display the warning in full: their end is destruction, their god their own appetites, their glory their shame, and their mind set wholly on earthly things — the opposite, in every way, of the heavenly-minded believer. The verse displays the ruin of the earthly-minded, against the believer's heavenly citizenship (v.20).
20:
"For our conversation is in heaven; from whence also we look for the Saviour, the Lord Jesus Christ:" The witnesses note the believer's citizenship in heaven and waiting for the Saviour. On "our conversation is in heaven," the grammatical voices note the citizenship. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with the earthly-minded: "our conversation (politeuma — citizenship, commonwealth, the state to which we belong) is in heaven" — believers are citizens of heaven, their true home and allegiance there, not on earth.

On "from whence also we look for the Saviour," the witnesses note the expectation. Barnes and Calvin note that "from whence (from heaven) also we look for (eagerly await, expect) the Saviour, the Lord Jesus Christ" — believers wait for Christ's return from heaven; their hope is set on his coming.

The Reformed Calvin notes the comfort: while the enemies of the cross mind earthly things, the believer's heart and hope are in heaven, whence the Saviour will come; the heavenly citizenship governs the whole of the Christian's life and longing. The verse displays the believer's citizenship in heaven.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast with the earthly-minded — "our conversation (citizenship, commonwealth) is in heaven," believers citizens of heaven, their home and allegiance there (Barnes, Robertson); "from whence (from heaven) we look for (eagerly await) the Saviour, the Lord Jesus Christ," waiting for Christ's return (Barnes, Calvin); the believer's heart and hope in heaven, governing the whole life (Calvin). The verse displays heavenly citizenship. The verse marks the citizenship: in heaven. The verse displays the home: there. The verse displays the expectation: the Saviour. The verse displays the comfort: hope above. The citizenship displays believers awaiting Christ from heaven. The verse displays the conversation: in heaven. The verse displays the looking: for the Saviour. The verse displays the Lord: Jesus Christ. The believer's citizenship in heaven and waiting for the Saviour display the great contrast with the earthly-minded enemies: the believer's true commonwealth is in heaven, whence he eagerly awaits the Saviour, the Lord Jesus Christ — his home, allegiance, and hope all set above. The verse displays the heavenly hope, crowned by the transformation of the body (v.21).
21:
"Who shall change our vile body, that it may be fashioned like unto his glorious body, according to the working whereby he is able even to subdue all things unto himself." The witnesses note the transformation of the body into the likeness of Christ's glorious body. On "who shall change our vile body," the grammatical voices note the transformation. Barnes and Robertson note that the returning Saviour "shall change (transform, refashion) our vile body (the body of our humiliation — lowly, frail, mortal, subject to decay)" — at his coming the believer's lowly body will be transformed.

On "fashioned like unto his glorious body," the witnesses note the pattern. Barnes and Calvin note that the body will be "fashioned like unto (conformed to) his glorious body (the body of his glory)" — made like Christ's own risen, glorified body; the believer's resurrection body will share the likeness of Christ's.

On "according to the working whereby he is able even to subdue all things," the grammatical-historical note the power: this transformation is "according to the working (energeia — the energy, mighty operation) whereby he is able even to subdue (subject) all things unto himself" — the same almighty power by which Christ governs and will subdue all things will refashion the believer's body; his omnipotence guarantees the resurrection. The verse displays the transformation of the body into the likeness of Christ's glorious body.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the returning Saviour "shall change (transform) our vile body (the body of our humiliation — frail, mortal)" (Barnes, Robertson); "fashioned like unto (conformed to) his glorious body," made like Christ's risen, glorified body (Barnes, Calvin); "according to the working (mighty power) whereby he is able even to subdue all things," the same omnipotence that governs all guaranteeing the resurrection (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the body transformed. The verse marks the change: our vile body. The verse displays the pattern: his glorious body. The verse displays the power: subduing all things. The verse displays the guarantee: his omnipotence. The transformation displays the lowly body made like Christ's glory. The verse displays the vile body: changed. The verse displays the glorious body: the pattern. The verse displays the power: almighty. The transformation of the body into the likeness of Christ's glorious body displays the crown of the believer's heavenly hope: at his coming the Saviour will transform our lowly, mortal body into the likeness of his own glorious body — by the same almighty power with which he subdues all things to himself. The verse displays the glorious resurrection of the body, closing the chapter on the heavenly hope and calling the church to stand fast (4:1).
